source.title: Allostatic self-efficacy: a metacognitive theory of dyshomeostasis-induced fatigue and depression

confidence_rationale: Stephan et al. 2016 provide a hierarchical Bayesian framework in which depression emerges from persistent interoceptive prediction errors that shift metacognitive beliefs toward allostatic self-inefficacy: the agent believes its internal regulation will fail, withdraws from effortful approach behaviors, and enters a state of behavioral quiescence that is functionally protective but catastrophically disabling. This is precisely Q-021's Pathway A clinical analog: when drive regulation fails or is chronically violated, the agent converges on low-action policy as the minimum-prediction-error strategy. The behavioral flatness is not a bug -- it is the optimal policy for an agent whose internal model predicts that approach will not reduce drive (self-inefficacy). Confidence 0.73 because this is a theoretical framework paper, not primary experimental data, and the connection to REE's pure-avoidance training requires additional inferential steps.
failure_signatures: If depressive behavioral quiescence were primarily driven by excessive harm-avoidance (heightened NoGo signals) rather than by failure of approach drives, Q-021's Pathway A framing would be incomplete and this paper's self-efficacy framework would not map cleanly.

If agents with intact approach drives but disrupted homeostatic prediction (high interoceptive prediction error) did not show behavioral flatness, the interoceptive inference pathway to quiescence would be empirically unsupported.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Chronic interoceptive prediction error (dyshomeostasis) leads to a metacognitive update: the agent infers low allostatic self-efficacy -- the belief that regulatory actions will fail to restore homeostasis. Under low self-efficacy, the agent reduces effortful approach behaviors because these are predicted to incur cost without achieving drive reduction. The resulting behavioral profile is fatigue and depression -- reduced initiation, reduced goal pursuit, quiescent policy -- as an adaptive response to perceived self-inefficacy.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-021 asks whether training with harm-avoidance signals only produces behavioral flatness. Stephan et al. provide a complementary clinical model of how behavioral flatness can emerge from homeostatic drive failure: when the drive system fails to predict that approach will be rewarded (self-efficacy failure), the optimal policy converges on non-action. In REE, training with only harm signals is analogous to a regime where the drive/approach system has zero weight: the agent has no representation that actions can reduce drive or move toward goals. The minimum-harm policy is then also the minimum-action policy -- quiescence. The Stephan framework supports Q-021 by showing that in biological systems, disruption of the drive-reward prediction pathway (here via interoceptive dysregulation) directly produces behavioral flatness, independent of whether harm-avoidance systems are intact.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Stephan et al. 2016 is a theoretical framework paper about depression and fatigue as metacognitive responses to allostatic failure -- it does not study the effects of pure harm-avoidance training in a neural network. The connection to Q-021 requires treating 'allostatic self-inefficacy' as an analog to 'no approach drive training signal.' These are related but not identical: self-inefficacy involves actively predicting that approach will fail, whereas pure harm-avoidance training simply never installs an approach signal. The paper also mixes drive failure (homeostatic signal absent) with belief failure (belief about efficacy updated), which are architecturally distinct in REE.
mapping.source_context: Theoretical review; Frontiers in Human Neuroscience; Bayesian/active inference framing; draws on clinical depression and fatigue literature; no primary experimental data.

confidence_components.notes: Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, reputable journal. Source quality good but theoretical. Mapping fidelity moderate -- the self-efficacy framework maps to behavioral flatness under drive failure, which is adjacent to but not identical to pure harm-avoidance training. Transfer risk moderate-high because the clinical mechanism (interoceptive dysregulation and metacognitive belief update) differs from the REE training regime question.
